name: Maren After the Watcher
description: The Watcher reached home position. Maren's three years of tracking are complete. She wants to compare her final constellation data with Yael's Spire emission record to determine the exact moment of full network coherence — and what happens when it arrives.
accept_text: She looks different from the day before — not diminished, but finished, in the way of something that has completed its purpose and is now figuring out what it is without the task that defined it. Three years of tracking have ended. The Watcher is home. She has a notebook full of data and nothing immediately pressing to do with it, which for a person like Maren is disorienting in a specific way. 'The night readings.' She holds up the final notebook. 'Complete sequence from first movement detection to home position. Three years, seven months, and twelve days of continuous observation.' She pauses. 'The rate of movement plotted against the Grid recovery timeline gives me a very specific function. The Watcher's movement was not linear — it tracked the Grid's coherence curve. When the Grid healed faster, the Watcher moved faster. When the network stalled, the Watcher's movement slowed.' She looks at the notebook. 'I can calculate, from the movement curve, the Grid's healing rate at every point in the three-year period. And I can calculate, from where the healing rate is now, how long until full coherence.' She looks at the Spire direction. 'Yael has three months of Spire emission data with its own coherence curve. If our datasets match — if the Watcher's movement curve and the Spire's output curve are the same curve — then we have the Grid's healing timeline from two independent measurements and we can calculate the exact date of full coherence.' She hands you the notebook. 'Take this to Yael. Ask her to overlay my movement rate data against her emission density data.' She pauses. 'And ask her what happens to the Spire on the day full coherence arrives. The Spire has been broadcasting into an incomplete network for three hundred years. What does it do when the network is complete.'
complete_text: Yael takes the notebook and spends several minutes at her instruments before she speaks. She is not checking — she is already seeing it. 'The same curve.' She says it with the quiet conviction of someone whose independent measurement has just confirmed another independent measurement. 'The movement rate and the output density are tracking the same underlying function.' She looks at the comparison. 'The Grid's healing rate. Both of us measuring it from different positions and different phenomena, and the result is: the curve is accelerating. Not at a constant rate — it's building. Each improvement in coherence enables faster improvement in coherence.' She looks at the Spire. 'If this acceleration pattern continues at the current rate — and I have no reason to believe it will slow — the Grid reaches full coherence in approximately...' She works the calculation. 'Fourteen months.' She is quiet. 'Give or take the uncertainty in the measurement endpoints.' She looks at the instruments. 'What happens to the Spire.' She says this as though she has been working out the answer for a long time. 'The Spire has been the network's only active node for three hundred years. It has been broadcasting into silence and receiving silence back, except for the last eight months when the primary node reactivated and the network began responding.' She looks at the crystal. 'When the network reaches full coherence, the Spire is no longer the only active node. It becomes one node of many, operating as it was designed to operate — as part of a network rather than as its only support.' She pauses. 'What the Spire does that day: it rests. For the first time in three hundred years, the network doesn't need it to carry everything. It can share the load.' She looks at the crystal. 'I have been thinking of the Spire as a mechanism. I think I've been wrong about that.' She is quiet. 'Fourteen months. Tell Maren: fourteen months.'
